answer:
We need \(a_n\) to be the smallest positive multiple of 23 satisfying \(a_n \equiv 1 \pmod{2^n}\).  
This means \(a_n\) is the least positive solution to \(23k \equiv 1 \pmod{2^n}\), i.e., \(k \equiv 23^{-1} \pmod{2^n}\).  
Since \(23\) is odd, its inverse modulo \(2^n\) exists and is unique modulo \(2^n\); thus \(a_n\) is exactly that inverse reduced to the range \(1\) to \(2^n \cdot 23\) but minimal positive multiple of 23.

The condition \(a_n = a_{n+1}\) occurs when the solution modulo \(2^{n+1}\) is the same as modulo \(2^n\), which happens exactly when \(23^{-1} \bmod 2^n\) is already congruent to 1 modulo \(2^{n+1}\).  
This is equivalent
